dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 for a breathing difficulty at 408---Redacted--
Hi, I'm Mike. Are you the one who called 911?
Yes, I've struggled to breathe all day and developed chest pain two hours ago.
Can you tell me your name and what day it is today?
It's Mary, and we're on Wednesday, I believe.
What city are we in today, Mary?
We're in Richmond which I've lived in for years now.
You mentioned shortness of breath getting worse. Any other symptoms like difficulty swallowing or a coughing fit?

No, just the chest discomfort when I lie down. It radiates a little bit up here too. 10. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; EMT: You mentioned using oxygen and an inhaler-did you take any other medications for it? Do you mind if we check your oxygen level?
She's on home oxygen at 4L. Her saturation is 91% now, so it's on the low side, but we're getting it up as best we can.

She's also on medication for kidney disease and blood pressure, including some for pulmonary hypertension and chronic pain. We can review the list if needed, but she mentioned she's up to date with her current meds and dialysis schedule this week.
I see in your history you've had a prior stroke-any new numbness or weakness since then?
Her airway appears open and patent. She's speaking in short sentences due to her rapid breathing, and we're getting equal breath sounds bilaterally on auscultation as well.
Her respirations are rapid and shallow, at about 40 per minute. She is having some difficulty moving air while talking, and bases her shortness of breath worsening with any movement or laying flat.
Her breathing rate is 40, so definitely tachypneic. She is showing some increased work of breathing, and has difficulty speaking in sentences. .
She's in sinus tachycardia with a pulse of 120. Strong and regular per palpation. Skin is warm and dry with normal color, no diaphoresis observed. She is not pale at this time.
Pulse is 120, remains regular rhythm, and she's alert. .
Blood pressure is 197 over 124. She's definitely hypertensive but her skin remains warm, dry, and without pallor.
Oxygen saturation has climbed to 99% on 4L oxygen, so we're maintaining that for now and moving forward with the EKG monitoring as per protocol.
She's alert and responsive to direct questions, oriented to person, place, and time.
We measured her end-tidal CO2 at 20 with a normal waveform. That supports her respiratory effort so far.
She reports the pain as level 4 on a scale of 10 and it's localized to the chest. It doesn't seem sharp but dull and constant when she lays down or moves.
Her second blood pressure reading comes in at 201/123. Hypertension is the largest concern right now.
Alright, we are attaching the 4 lead and preparing the 12 lead EKG for implementation. She is already on 4L oxygen, and we are preparing to obtain a full tracing now. Please hold still for a moment.
EKG is on. At this time, we are picking up left bundle branch block. .
Her breath sounds are clear on both sides, consistent throughout all fields.
The 12 lead shows left bundle branch block with abnormal left axis deviation.
We also note some data quality concerns limiting full ECG analysis.
Protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress, considering exacerbation of COPD and abnormal EKG findings. .
We will recheck vitals now. .
VCU Hospital will be the destination because it has the best renal and pulmonary care available. She is aware of the destination. .
She is ready for transport. Straps and rails are fully engaged. She is upright on the stretcher with oxygen on and stable. .

We are handing over to the hospital team. She is receiving 4L oxygen via nasal cannula, remains alert, and EKG shows LBBB. No IV access or medications administered because of precautions for dialysis port. .
En route with stable vitals. Her BP was last checked at 201/123, pulse 120, SpO2 99%, and breathing rate 40. .
Patient arrived stable, on 4L oxygen via nasal cannula. No major changes in condition en route. She remained alert and oriented throughout. .
We noted in several EKG readings a possible apical infarct in some leads and right axis deviation in others. .
Glucose levels were unmeasurable in both checks. .
Formal handoff includes full vitals, history of stroke, current medications, dialysis status, and EKG findings. .
She has no evidence of edema or other major findings in secondary assessments aside from tachyperia and tachypnea. .
She has a history of CVA but no new changes in orientation or movement reported at this time. .
We did not observe any new neurological deficits or feel that stroke was a current concern. .
We observed no intercostal or sternal retractions, so no findings of severe respiratory distress palpable in superficial inspection. .
She has no known allergies and is maintained on regular medications for kidney disease, COPD, and pulmonary hypertension. .
She has a subclavian access for dialysis, and we followed the aerosolization protocols for suspected COV-19 case. .
She has not taken any nebulizers. .
All our actions remain within paramedical standing orders for suspected respiratory distress with abnormal EKG findings. .
She has never experienced dizziness or syncope, and is able to maintain her own airway and breathing despite tachypnea. .
We do not report any new numbness or weakness at this time over and above prior stroke history. .
Patient is now fully oriented and secured for the transport to VCU Hospital for further evaluation. .
No IV fluids administered, any procedures documented in our field notes for the attending staff at the facility. .
Complete documentation is being handed over now along with EKG interpretation to the hospital team received via radio. .
Level 4 patient condition for the hospital team, stable from last vitals per normal. .
Patient has increased work of breathing with no new muscle tone or extremity positioning concerns. .
She is currently on 4L of home oxygen via nasal cannula, which is working best for her. .
Breathing taken at 40-41 with no significant deviation in rhythm or volume observed on last check. .
She is hypertensive and not taking any at-home IV medications related to her condition today. .
Final EKG strips obtained for submission with patient. .
ECG findings noted include measurement issues with some leads, but no further acute life threatening findings observed on current passing. .
We have no new patient reportable tachycardia or arrhythmias observed beyond baseline here. .
Skin appears unremarkable and warm with no significant rashes, bruising, or pallor noted in inspection. .
All actions overviewed for facility with realistic pacing and event accounting. .
She was admitted to VCU Health yesterday with similar concerns and awaiting any results on COV-19 testing at time of call. .
She had no injuries, wounds, or small strokes reported in supplementary assessments. .
No additional communication with other parties involved in this case at this time. .
We have not placed the patient on any continuous posturing for comfort or airway support. .
Her status remains stable per latest observations. .
We have not obtained suprapubic measure for any related concerns. .
Any bowel, pelvic variations remain up to internal facilities for further workup. .
She was able to recall both short and long term memories during mental status check. .
GCS was 15 and current status unchanged during exit interview. .
She is not currently in distress or in need of immediate ICU intervention at this time. .
No vomit or adverse movements observed to be inputted apart from patient report. .
Her last food intake was on prior day as reported. .
She has no current event liability at point of handoff. .
Remaining patient records in current possession with no sequelae reported. .
Her personal ID and updated medication list reviewed and handed off accordingly. .
No specific movement is reported for back, shoulder or joint response per final check. .
We confirmed no bite marks, rashes, or irritation noted on skin exam. .
She has no reported allergies to food, insect stings, or medications at this time. .
No asterisks, airway mandates, or stabbing injuries found on current fitness. .
Her current unconsciousness is non-existent per our evaluation. .
She was not part of any accident or trauma collision at this time. .
Kidney failure status continues to inform transport and medication actions. .
All emergency access arrangements finalized for handing off at destination. .
No IV in place due to dialysis line and aerosolization risk, and no nebulized meds were given. .
She is otherwise unremarkable in torso and limb positioning for stable transport. .
She has not described any new rash, shortness of breath, or vomiting symptoms at any interval. .
No prior traumatic history evident from documentation for this case or transport staff. .
Interventions were non-invasive and within scope of provision for emergency transport. .
No upper airway complaints, throat symptoms, or associated issues found on transport setup. .
She refused antihistamines and epinephrine suggested therapy for allergic response this time. .
Patient has not taken any sexual protective actions or needs for current report. .
No psychotropic meds taken at the scene to threshold hospital services in any way. .
No emetic contents nor substance abuse allegations currently actionable at this stage. .
All safety protocols recalled for trail and beyond. .
Final clearance performed with technological and visual inspection of correct equipment for task. .
All clinical changes yet to manifest nothing pending immediate hospital discovery. .
There are no airway or mechanical interventions required at this stage. .
Per her, no further issues planned ahead after transport phase from current station.
